title: Secretary General Jens Stoltenberg Congressional Gold Medal Act
short_title: Secretary General Jens Stoltenberg Congressional Gold Medal Act
plain_summary: This bill gives a special gold medal from Congress to Jens Stoltenberg, who is the leader of NATO. NATO is a group of countries that work together to keep each other safe. Congress wants to honor Stoltenberg because he has done a good job leading NATO and helping the countries stay united and secure. The bill also says the Treasury can make bronze copies of the medal to sell to people, and the money from those sales will go back to the U.S. Mint.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Secretary General Jens Stoltenberg Congressional Gold Medal Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Findings
sections.text: Congress has found the following facts. Jens Stoltenberg was the Prime Minister of Norway from two thousand to two thousand one and two thousand five to two thousand thirteen. Norway was one of the original members of NATO (a military alliance called the North Atlantic Treaty Organization) when it was created on April fourth, nineteen forty nine. When Jens Stoltenberg was Prime Minister of Norway, he helped his country spend more money on defense and upgrade its military. NATO's main goal is to keep member countries safe and support democracy to maintain peace and stability. Jens Stoltenberg became the Secretary General (the top leader) of NATO in October two thousand fourteen. The United States was the first NATO member to support his appointment to this job. Under his leadership, NATO has made major new investments, strengthened its abilities, and improved the shared defense of all member countries. Jens Stoltenberg has pushed for all NATO members to pay their fair share of defense costs. Because of his work, twenty three member countries will meet or exceed the goal of spending two percent of their national income on defense by two thousand twenty four. This compares to only four countries that met this goal in two thousand fourteen. His efforts to get countries to share the burden fairly has made NATO stronger and more sustainable than ever before. While Jens Stoltenberg has been the leader, NATO has grown bigger several times and added Finland, Montenegro, North Macedonia and Sweden as new members. Adding new members not only makes the alliance more secure, but also shows that more countries are meeting the military, political and legal requirements needed to join NATO. This improves cooperation, defense spending and intelligence sharing among member countries. Jens Stoltenberg has strengthened NATO's partnerships with countries in the Indo-Pacific region (like Japan and Australia) to work together on shared challenges including cyber defense, new technologies, and problems caused by China. He invited Indo-Pacific leaders to NATO meetings and traveled to their region to strengthen these important partnerships. After Russia launched a full scale invasion of Ukraine in February two thousand twenty two, Jens Stoltenberg has kept NATO members united against Putin's unprovoked and illegal actions. Since February two thousand twenty two, NATO members have given Ukraine the equipment and resources it needs to defend its democracy and independence. Jens Stoltenberg successfully got political and financial support from Indo-Pacific partners to help Ukraine, including donations of weapons and military equipment and large financial contributions to NATO's trust fund for Ukraine assistance. NATO allies unanimously agreed to extend Jens Stoltenberg's term four different times, with two extensions happening after Russia invaded Ukraine. Jens Stoltenberg is the second longest serving Secretary General, having served more than nine years in this position. Jens Stoltenberg has promised that NATO will stand together against any threat to any NATO member, ensuring continued peace and stability in NATO territory and around the world.

sections.title: Section 3 Congressional Gold Medal
sections.text: The Speaker of the House of Representatives and the President pro tempore of the Senate (the temporary president of the Senate) will make proper arrangements to award a single gold medal to Jens Stoltenberg on behalf of Congress. This medal recognizes his contributions to the security, unity, and defense of NATO. The Secretary of the Treasury will create and make a gold medal with suitable symbols, designs, and writing that the Secretary decides on. The design must include the name of Jens Stoltenberg.

sections.title: Section 4 Duplicate Medals
sections.text: The Secretary of the Treasury may make and sell bronze copies of the gold medal at a price that covers all the costs of making the medals. These costs include labor, materials, the metal dies used to stamp the medals, use of machinery, and overhead expenses.

sections.title: Section 5 Status of Medals
sections.text: All medals made under this law are considered national medals according to chapter fifty one of title thirty one of the United States Code (a section of federal law). For the purposes of sections five thousand one hundred thirty four and five thousand one hundred thirty six of title thirty one of the United States Code, all medals made under this law will be considered numismatic items (collectible coins and medals).

sections.title: Section 6 Authority to Use Fund Amounts and Proceeds of Sale
sections.text: The government is authorized to charge the United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund for whatever amounts are needed to pay for the costs of making the medals under this law. Money received from selling the duplicate bronze medals will be put back into the United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund.
